old_claim.note: Leather Working provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct leather_working edge remains because parchment and vellum are prepared animal-skin writing supports, not tanned leather goods; the corrected dependency is hide preparation and finishing.

source_shape.source_locator: National Archives describes parchment manufacture as removing the skin, cleaning away hair and flesh, stretching it on a wooden frame, scraping, wetting, drying, and finishing the surface for ink.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports prepared-skin processing for writing surfaces rather than tanned leather working as the direct craft dependency.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Leather working can be adjacent to later bound-book covers or other hide crafts, but the parchment/vellum part of this node depends on cleaned, stretched, scraped, and finished skins for writing.

ontology_before: Papyrus & Parchment Writing Surfaces directly depended on Leather Working, implying parchment was a leather-working product.
ontology_after: Papyrus & Parchment Writing Surfaces depends on hide_processing for the parchment/vellum branch while keeping leather_working out of the direct prerequisite set.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: leather_working is no longer a direct prerequisite for paper_making_papyrus_parchment.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed specifically to leather book covers or leather goods rather than writing surfaces.
A stronger source showed the first-known parchment/vellum writing-surface process required tanning or leather-working techniques instead of parchment preparation.
A separate leather-covered-codex node were being audited, where leather_working may remain a direct material/craft prerequisite.
short_reason: Parchment and vellum are prepared skins for writing, not tanned leather-working products.
